coredata.dc:description: The extensive use of organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs), particularly chlorpyrifos (CP), has raised significant environmental and health concerns, necessitating effective sensing and removal from real samples and wastewater. This study reports the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposites, highlighting their effectiveness in fluorescence-based sensing and photocatalytic degradation of CP under direct sunlight. The N-CQDs exhibited fluorescence with a quantum yield of 44.48 %, enabling the detection of CP with an LOD of 0.084 ppm. Practical applicability was demonstrated in wastewater and agricultural runoff samples. The N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite, synthesized via a green approach utilizing renewable resources, achieved 91 % degradation of CP within 70 min under sunlight exposure. Hydroxyl radicals were identified as key contributors to the degradation process, with GC-MS analysis revealing the formation of safe metabolites. The nanocomposite exhibited excellent reusability over six cycles, underscoring its potential as an eco-friendly solution for pesticide contamination.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The extensive use of organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs), particularly chlorpyrifos (CP), has raised significant environmental and health concerns, necessitating effective sensing and removal from real samples and wastewater. This study reports the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposites, highlighting their effectiveness in fluorescence-based sensing and photocatalytic degradation of CP under direct sunlight. The N-CQDs exhibited fluorescence with a quantum yield of 44.48 %, enabling the detection of CP with an LOD of 0.084 ppm. Practical applicability was demonstrated in wastewater and agricultural runoff samples. The N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite, synthesized via a green approach utilizing renewable resources, achieved 91 % degradation of CP within 70 min under sunlight exposure. Hydroxyl radicals were identified as key contributors to the degradation process, with GC-MS analysis revealing the formation of safe metabolites. The nanocomposite exhibited excellent reusability over six cycles, underscoring its potential as an eco-friendly solution for pesticide contamination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticide-induced water pollution remains a critical environmental challenge, with organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs), particularly chlorpyrifos (CP), emerging as major contributors. Chlorpyrifos, extensively utilized for its insecticidal and herbicidal efficacy, poses substantial threats to aquatic ecosystems and public health [1–5]. This compound exhibits distinctive physicochemical properties, including a crystalline structure, a faint mercaptan odor, aqueous solubility of 1.2 mg/L at 25 °C, and a soil half-life ranging from 10 to 120 days [6–9]. Although it remains stable under acidic and neutral conditions, CP undergoes hydrolysis in strong alkaline environments, forming toxic by-products such as chlorpyrifos oxon, which exhibits enhanced toxicity [10–13].

The extensive application of CP has precipitated significant public health concerns, including neurological disorders, genotoxicity, oxidative stress, and systemic organ damage [14–18]. CP operates as an acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitor, disrupting neurotransmission and leading to severe neurological symptoms, convulsions, and, in extreme cases, mortality [19–21]. Furthermore, CP induces genotoxic effects, reduces cellular viability, and exacerbates oxidative damage to critical organs such as the brain and liver. Chronic exposure is associated with long-term health complications, including hepatotoxicity, carcinogenesis, nephrotoxicity, reproductive dysfunction, and neurodegeneration [22–28].

The detection of CP in environmental and food matrices is fraught with challenges, given its widespread utilization and the intricacies of multi-residue analysis [29–31]. Conventional analytical techniques such as liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) offer high sensitivity and specificity. However, these methods are often constrained by high operational costs, labor-intensive procedures, and the complexity of sample preparation [32]. These limitations underscore the need for innovative detection methodologies that are both cost-effective and environmentally sustainable [33].

Leveraging agricultural byproducts for the synthesis of CQDs represents a sustainable and cost-efficient approach. Kinnow (Citrus reticulata) peels, characterized by their high carbon content, serve as an optimal precursor for green synthesis, aligning with the principles of the circular economy [34]. The incorporation of nitrogen atoms during synthesis further enhances the electronic and optical properties of CDs, such as fluorescence quantum yield and surface reactivity, thereby augmenting their suitability for sensing and catalytic applications [35].

Metal hexacyanoferrates (MHCFs), notably Prussian blue analogues, have demonstrated considerable potential in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants, attributable to their low band gaps and environmentally benign nature. Among these, zinc hexacyanoferrate (ZnHCF) exhibits promising photocatalytic properties, which can be significantly enhanced through doping strategies that improve charge carrier dynamics and expand active surface areas [36–39] Recent advancements have highlighted the synergistic integration of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with ZnHCF to enhance photocatalytic efficiency, facilitating effective CP degradation under solar irradiation.

This research emphasizes the green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) from Kinnow peels using a microwave-assisted methodology. These N-CQDs were utilized for the detection of CP, benefiting from their superior fluorescence and sensitivity relative to traditional CDs. Additionally, a nanocomposite comprising N-CQDs and ZnHCF was engineered to achieve efficient photodegradation of CP under sunlight. The N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite exhibited remarkable efficiency in degrading CP in complex matrices, such as wastewater and agricultural runoff. This work introduces a novel paradigm in addressing pesticide contamination, combining sustainable synthesis with advanced functionalization to deliver a holistic and eco-friendly solution for CP detection and remediation in aquatic environments.

Fresh Kinnow (Citrus reticulata) fruit was collected directly from a local fruit seller. All pesticides, such as Atrazine (AT), P-Cresol (PC), Thiamethoxam (TMX), Fipronil (FPN), Indoxcarb (IDC), and Chlorpyrifos (CP), were procured from Sigma Aldrich, USA. At the same time, zinc nitrate, potassium hexacyanoferrate, urea, and ammonium hydroxide were sourced from LOBA Chemie (analytical grade). Deionized water was used as the solvent for all the experimental methods. Methanol with a minimum purity of 99.9 %, acetonitrile (ACN) with a minimum purity of 99.9 %, and dichloromethane (DCM) with a minimum purity of 99.9 % were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. These chemicals are of HPLC quality.

5 g of kinnow (Citrus reticulata) peels were thoroughly washed with DI water to remove dust and impurities. The peels were then dried and ground into a fine powder. A suspension solution was prepared by dissolving urea (2 g) in DI water (10 mL), which was then added to the powdered peels to dope the peels. This mixture was combined with 40 mL of DI water and exposed to microwave irradiation at 800 W for 10 min. Afterward, 20 mL of DI water was added, and the mixture was filtered and dialyzed against 40 mL of distilled water for 48 h to remove any unreacted material (see Scheme 1a). The synthesized N-CQDs were stored at 4 °C for further applications.

The aqueous solution (0.1 M, 100 mL) of zinc nitrate and (0.05 M, 100 mL) of potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]) was stirred with 2 mL of green tea extract. Green tea extract functions as both a reducing and stabilizing agent in the green synthesis of nanoparticles, attributable to its high content of polyphenols, flavonoids, and other bioactive compounds. These phytochemicals are crucial in reducing metal ions while simultaneously stabilizing the synthesized nanoparticles, promoting eco-friendly and efficient synthesis methodologies. To keep the pH around 12, dropwise addition of liq. Ammonia solution (20 mL) was used and stirred for 2 h. The solution was then undisturbed for 24 h, and the precipitates were filtered using 0.22 μm filter paper. The solid precipitates were then washed with deionized water to remove the unreacted parts, dried in a hot air oven (60 °C, 12 h), and calcinated in a muffle furnace (200 °C, 2 h).

The aqueous solution (0.1 M, 100 mL) of zinc nitrate and (0.05 M, 100 mL) of potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]) was stirred with 2 mL of N-CQDs solution and 2 mL of green tea extract. To keep the pH around 12, dropwise addition of liq. Ammonia solution (20 mL) was used and stirred for 2 h. The solution was then undisturbed for 24 h, and the precipitates were filtered using 0.22 μm filter paper (see Scheme 1b). The solid precipitates were then washed with deionized water to remove the unreacted parts, dried in a hot air oven (60 °C, 12 h), and calcinated in a muffle furnace (200 °C, 2 h).

To explore the selectivity of N-CQDs towards pesticides based on their FL behavior, a mixture combining 100-ppm (1 mL) pesticide solution was prepared with 25 μL of N-CQDs solution. Fluorescence intensities were then measured after a 15-min equilibration period. The experiment was repeated using varying pesticide concentrations (CP) ranging from 1 to 100 ppm with N-CQDs under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental setup for photodegradation of CP
ce:section.ce:para:
The efficiency of N-CQDs, ZnHCF, and N-CQDs@ZnHCF in degrading CP from simulated water under sunlight irradiation was assessed. Various concentrations of CP (20–60 mg L−1), catalyst amounts (5–25 mg), pH levels (3–11), and different time intervals were studied. Each pollutant concentration (15 mL solution) underwent treatment with nanocatalysts. Citrate buffer and borax were used to maintain a consistent pH. Sunlight irradiation occurred for 2 h (11:00 a.m. to 2:30 p.m.), with ambient conditions monitored (452 ± 184 lux, 30 ± 1.7 °C). Additionally, samples were incubated in darkness for one hour to assess adsorption properties. At regular intervals, samples were taken, spun in a centrifuge, and immediately analyzed using a UV–visible spectrometer. The degrading efficiency (%) of the photocatalyst was calculated by applying the equation:
(1)
% Degrdation =
Ci − Cf
Ci
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comprehensive characterization: structural, optical, and elemental insights

ce:section.ce:para: High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analyses were utilized to examine the surface structure, size distribution, and elemental composition of N-CQDs. TEM images ( Fig. 1a) revealed a spherical morphology, consistent with other studies on CQDs, which typically exhibit similar shapes due to their graphitic nature [40]. FE-SEM and EDS techniques were used to analyze the morphological features, particle size, and elemental content of the fabricated NPs and nanocomposite. It was observed that ZnHCF NPs have a spherical morphology, while N-CQDs@ZnHCF have a distorted spherical morphology (Fig. 1b, c). FE-SEM images of N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposites demonstrate the incorporation of N-CQDs into the ZnHCF matrix, a critical factor in enhancing the composite’s functional properties [41,42]. HR-TEM images (Fig. 1g) demonstrated a narrow size distribution ranging from 2 to 7 nm, with an average diameter of 4.45 ± 1.14 nm, calculated using ImageJ software. These results confirm the monodisperse nature of N-CQDs, aligning with findings in other works [43]. The average particle size of ZnHCF and N-CQDs@ZnHCF was determined to be 40.46 ± 4.9 and 40.58 ± 5.5 nm, respectively (Fig. 1h, i), which falls within the expected range for such nanomaterials [44].

The EDS analysis (Fig. 1d) indicated that N-CQDs comprised 42.74 % carbon, 24.75 % nitrogen, and 27.55 % oxygen, suggesting successful doping. This finding corroborates previous reports demonstrating the efficacy of nitrogen doping in modifying the electronic properties of CQDs [45,46]. The elemental makeup of N-CQDs, ZnHCF, and N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposites (Fig. 1e, f) revealed that N-CQDs contain 42.74 % carbon, 24.75 % nitrogen, and 27.55 % oxygen. ZnHCF exhibited a composition of C (19.32 %), O (29.35 %), N (9.45 %), Fe (8.48 %), and Zn (33.40 %). In the case of the N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite, the composition comprised C (21.91 %), O (30.04 %), N (12.32 %), Fe (7.89 %), and Zn (27.84 %). This elemental analysis confirmed the expected compositions, validating these nanomaterials' successful synthesis and integration. The consistent elemental distribution of nitrogen and carbon in the nanocomposite indicates the successful incorporation of N-CQDs, which is crucial for enhancing the material’s catalytic and sensing properties [47,48].

XPS analysis was conducted to determine the elemental makeup of the N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite. Five main elements are there in the XPS, i.e., Zn, Fe, C, O, and N. The presence of many chemical states of Fe and Zn results in multiple contributions with distinct binding energies in the XPS. Fig. 2 shows the XPS survey and individual elemental spectra. The N 1 s spectrum with binding energies of 397.7 eV [49]. These values indicate the presence of nitrogen in the nanocomposite material [50]. The Fe 2p1/2 state is responsible for the peak at binding energies (B.E.) of 716.4 eV. The two peaks observed in the Zn 2p spectrum, corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, are located at binding energies of 1022.52 eV and 1044.51 eV, respectively, and the energy difference between the two peaks is 21.99 eV, which is consistent with previous reports [50]. The O 1 s peak resolves to a single peak at 532.5 eV, which may be attributed to lattice oxygen [51]. Furthermore, the deconvoluted C-1s peak at 286.4 eV was linked to C(1 s) in the nanocomposite [52,53]. Using a survey scan of elements, the following percentages of atoms were calculated: C (18.32 %), O (28.37 %), N (14.32 %), Zn (27.84 %), and Fe (6.79 %). Consequently, the XPS data indicates the formation of ZnHCF nanoparticles, reflecting the interaction between Zn and Fe. The XPS investigation shows that the N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite has a higher coverage percentage of N, C, and O; this conclusion is consistent with the results obtained from PXRD and EDS microscopy.

The XRD pattern of N-CQDs shows broad peaks characteristic of an amorphous structure ( Fig. 3a). This broadness is typical for CQDs, where distinct diffraction peaks are usually unobserved, confirming their amorphous nature [54,55]. Such characteristics have been consistently reported in studies involving carbon-based nanomaterials, where the lack of sharp peaks indicates small, disordered structures [56]. The sharp diffraction peaks observed in the XRD pattern of N-CQDs may be attributed to crystalline domains or graphitic regions within the primarily amorphous carbon structure. Nitrogen doping can further modify the carbon lattice, leading to localized crystalline zones, which are detectable by XRD. Similar findings have been reported in studies on heteroatom-doped CQDs, where doping enhances the crystallinity of certain regions within the material [57,58]. In contrast, the XRD analysis of ZnHCF reveals characteristic diffraction peaks corresponding to its crystalline structure. The peak at 2θ = 15° represents the (111) plane reflection of the cubic crystal lattice of ZnHCF, while the peak at 2θ = 16.6° corresponds to the (002) plane reflection, indicating the layered structure of the material. Additionally, the peak at 2θ = 25° is associated with the (220) plane reflection, further confirming the crystallinity of ZnHCF. These diffraction peaks confirm the successful creation of ZnHCF with a clear crystal structure, in line with previous studies on similar hexacyanoferrate materials [59,60]. When analyzing N-CQDs@ZnHCF, the XRD pattern displays more characteristics than pure ZnHCF, showing effective doping. A shifted peak at 2θ = 15.5° implies a modification in the interlayer spacing due to N-CQDs incorporation into the ZnHCF lattice, often observed in doped nanocomposites [61,62]. A new peak at 2θ = 30° indicates either additional crystalline phases' presence or new crystalline structures forming due to doping. The broadening of peaks associated with the ZnHCF lattice reflects a decrease in crystallite size or increased structural disorder caused by integrating N-CQDs, consistent with reports of doping-induced structural changes [63,64]. These observations of altered diffraction peaks demonstrate the successful integration of N-CQDs into the ZnHCF lattice, effectively modifying its structural properties and confirming the formation of a new nanocomposite material with potentially enhanced functional characteristics.

Fig. 3(b) shows the N-CQDs exhibit a broad peak near 3300–3400 cm⁻¹ in the FTIR spectrum. This peak is due to N-H and O-H stretching vibrations, signifying the presence of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CQDs [65,66]. Another significant peak at 1610 cm⁻¹ corresponds to CC or CO stretching, suggesting the existence of sp² hybridized carbon and potentially carbonyl groups, typical in carbon-based nanomaterials [67]. A noticeable peak at 1460 cm⁻¹ relates to C-H bending vibrations. Additionally, a peak at 1067 cm² is attributed to C-O stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of oxygenated functional groups on the N-CQD surface [68].

The FT-IR spectrum of ZnHCF reveals characteristic peaks confirming its structure. A distinct peak at 2096 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the CN stretching vibration, typical of cyanide groups, confirming their presence in the ZnHCF structure [69]. Additional peaks at 1460 cm⁻¹ are associated with C-H bending vibrations, while those at 600 cm⁻¹ and 554 cm⁻¹ indicate Zn-N stretching and Fe-C bonds, respectively, confirming coordination between zinc and nitrogen, and iron and carbon [70,71].

In contrast, the FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs@ZnHCF reveals differences following successful doping. The broad peak at 3400 cm⁻¹ indicates O-H/N-H stretching vibrations, suggesting the contribution of N-CQDs functional groups to the nanocomposite [72]. The CN stretching peak shifts slightly to 2109 cm⁻¹ , indicating a redshift, which suggests an interaction between N-CQDs and the cyanide groups within ZnHCF [73]. The preservation of carbonyl groups post-doping is reflected in the CO stretching peak at 1589 cm⁻¹ , showing that the core functional groups of N-CQDs remain intact within the composite.

Furthermore, the altered intensity in the Zn-N/Fe-C stretching peak at 594 cm⁻¹ indicates changes in the local environment around zinc and iron atoms, signifying successful doping. The broadening of the N-H/O-H stretching peak further supports enhanced hydrogen bonding interactions. The shift of the CN stretching peak and these spectral changes provide clear evidence of the successful incorporation of N-CQDs into the ZnHCF structure.

Additionally, The FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs and N-CQDs@ZnHCF were compared to confirm that the -CN bond is exclusive to ZnHCF. The absence of the -CN peak in the N-CQDs spectrum clearly demonstrates that this bond is not derived from N-CQDs. Furthermore, the sharper -CN peak in ZnHCF compared to the broader hump in N-CQDs provides additional evidence. This distinction is corroborated by XPS analysis, which identifies nitrogen species in the composite as characteristic of cyanide groups. The results confirm the successful integration of N-CQDs into the ZnHCF structure, along with distinct interactions between the components. The findings of Rishabh [74], whose XPS analysis showed no evidence of the -CN bond in N-CQDs, have been cited in the discussion to provide further context

Fig. S2a displays the FL emission spectra of N-CQDs when excited with wavelengths between 300 and 380 nm. The peak intensities of FL emission of N-CQDs exhibit wavelength dependence, which can be attributed to the distinct functional groups on the N-CQDs. These groups are excitation energy traps, resulting in defects [75,76]. The surface properties of N-CQDs, including their functional group composition, are associated with variants in FL emission. Additionally, surface modifications, particle size, and heteroatoms play a role [77,78]. When excited at 320 nm, the emission spectra exhibit a maximum intensity at 883 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs are displayed in (Fig. S2 b). Polycyclic aromatic structures' absorption is indicated as a peak at 276 nm. The inset highlights the strong blue fluorescence of the synthesized N-CQDs under UV light (365 nm excitation). The quantum yield was computed at 44.48 % using quinine sulfate as a reference (53%) to further assess the fluorescence properties [79,80].

The fluorescence emission spectra in Fig. S2c demonstrate the enhanced photophysical properties of the N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite compared to pure ZnHCF. While ZnHCF shows a weak emission peak around 450 nm (∼200 a.u.), the N-CQDs@ZnHCF displays a much stronger emission (∼700 a.u.) at the same 320 excitation wavelength, indicating improved charge separation and reduced electron-hole recombination due to the incorporation of N-CQDs. Additionally, the N-CQDs@ZnHCF spectrum reveals a secondary peak near 550 nm, absent in ZnHCF, suggesting the formation of new emissive states that enhance charge transport. The significant increase in fluorescence intensity, with N-CQDs@ZnHCF being over three times more intense than ZnHCF at 450 nm, confirms the material’s higher photogenerated charge separation efficiency. This reduction in electron-hole recombination directly correlates with the nanocomposite’s enhanced photocatalytic performance, making it promising for energy conversion and environmental remediation applications.

The characterization of band gap properties in nanoparticles involves employing UV–visible reflectance analysis. The band gap of ZnHCF is 2.3 eV (Fig. S2 d), but integrating N-CQDs into ZnHCF reduces it to 2.15 eV by altering the energy levels (Fig. S2 e). This significant change in the band gap indicates that incorporating N-CQDs effectively modifies the electronic properties of ZnHCF. Reducing the band gap could enhance the material's optical and electronic properties, potentially making it more suitable for applications in photocatalysis.

pH-dependent characteristics were examined by dilution: 25 μL of N-CQDs were diluted in 5 mL of DI water, and the mixture was whirled for 120 s. A pH meter measured The FL emission spectrum across a pH range of 3–10. The data, calibrated using buffer solutions resulting in pH 3 and 7, are displayed in Fig. S3a. This study analyzed acidic (pH 3–6) and basic (pH 8–10) conditions and found that the highest FL intensity was at pH 7. The FL intensity somewhat decreased as the pH changed from acidic to basic. The protonation and deprotonation behaviors of the N-CQDs are affected by the contact of H⁺ or OH⁻ ions with their functional groups, resulting in these alterations [81].

Looking into the long-term stability of N-CQDs, emission intensity was measured at a wavelength of 320 nm after 75 days of storage at 5 °C (Fig. S3 b). The results showed a minimal variation of less than 0.9 %, indicating that N-CQDs maintain stability over time. This suggests that N-CQDs hold promise for practical sensing applications and commercial use. Furthermore, photostability tests under UV light (365 nm) for 30 min (Fig. S3 c) revealed that the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs remained largely constant, confirming their robust photostability. Moreover, varying the concentration of KCl solution (0–2 M) assessed the impact of ionic strength on the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs. The results showed negligible differences in FL intensities across this range (Fig. S3 d), indicating that ionic strength has little effect on the fluorescence behavior of N-CQDs [82]. Overall, these findings emphasize the stable photoluminescent properties of N-CQDs under different conditions, such as varying pH levels, extended storage periods, and changes in ionic strengths. This stability highlights N-CQDs' potential for diverse applications in sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examining selectivity and quenching of fluorescence

ce:section.ce:para: To improve the practical use of the fluorescence quenching method, we optimized the effects of incubation time and pH, as shown in Fig. S3. The results revealed that changes in pH and incubation time had a minimal impact on the sensor system's FL quenching efficiency (F0/F). A pH of 7 and an incubation time of 6 min were selected to maintain consistency in subsequent experiments. N-CQDs were equilibrated with various solutions containing pesticides at a concentration of 100 ppm for the fluorescence detection of pesticides. This study, conducted at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, aimed to assess N-CQDs' selectivity towards various pesticides. We observed changes in FL intensity and quenching levels in the presence of different pesticides, including atrazine (AT), cresol (PC), thiamethoxam (TMX), fipronil (FPN), indoxcarb (IDC), and chlorpyrifos (CP), as shown in Fig. 4a.

Under the specified conditions, different concentrations of CP (ranging from 1 to 100 ppm) were added to the N-CQD solution. A linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching ratio (F0/F) and CP concentration was established, as illustrated in Fig. 4b. The regression equation, with a (Ksv) slope of 0.00686 and (R2) correlation coefficient of 0.9865, is shown in Fig. 4c. With the following equation, the limit of detection (LOD) for CP was determined to be 0.084 ppm:
(2) LOD = 3σ/m

In this case, σ represents the standard deviation of the regression lines' y-intercepts, and m is the calibration graph's slope. Additionally, Supporting Text 1.1 and Table S1 compares our approach's LOD with previously published CD-based sensors [82–88]. The comparison shows our method's advantages since it has a lower LOD than other approaches. This underscores our NCQD-based sensor system's high sensitivity and selectivity for pesticide detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction of CP with N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between N-CQDs and CP results in a significant fluorescence quenching, as shown in Fig. 4d. This quenching mechanism is supported by UV-Vis, fluorescence, and FTIR analyses, which reveal the specific binding interactions involved. N-CQDs possess functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), amine (-NH₂), and carbonyl (-CO) on their surface, which play a crucial role in interacting with the reactive sites of CP, including phosphoryl (P = O) and thiophosphoryl (P = S) groups (Fig. S4). These interactions, primarily through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding, form complexes between N-CQDs and CP (Fig. 4d). FTIR analysis confirms the interaction between N-CQDs and CP by identifying binding sites. Upon interaction with CP, subtle shifts in characteristic peaks were observed within the O-H/N-H stretching region (3200–3500 cm⁻¹) and the CO stretching peak (1580–1660 cm⁻¹), as previously reported [89]. This finding is consistent with reports by [90,91], who described similar binding interactions in CQD-based pesticide sensors. These shifts suggest hydrogen bonding occurs between the phosphoryl (P = O) and thiophosphoryl (P = S) groups of CP and the hydroxyl, amine, and carbonyl groups on the N-CQDs. Additionally, peaks in the C-N and C-O stretching regions (1000–1400 cm⁻¹) exhibit slight shifts and changes in intensity, indicating that both electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions are involved in the interaction between CP and N-CQDs, thus confirming the nature of these binding interactions (Fig. 4e).

Fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted to further support the dynamic quenching mechanism (Fig. S5 and Table S2), and the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs decreased from 4.64 ns to 3.71 ns upon adding CP. This reduction indicates non-radiative decay pathways induced by CP, supporting the dynamic quenching model proposed by [92]. The high sensitivity of the N-CQDs-based sensor (LOD = 0.084 ppm) demonstrates its superior performance compared to other reported CQD-based sensors [83]. The enhanced selectivity can be attributed to the tailored surface functional groups on N-CQDs, which facilitate specific interactions with CP over other pesticides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of CP recovery in different wastewater samples
ce:section.ce:para: A realistic implementation of N-CQDs for detecting CP was assessed by analyzing samples of contaminated wastewater and agricultural runoff. After treatment, the effluent was gathered from nearby treatment plants, while agricultural runoff was acquired from adjacent fields. To estimate recovery, the samples were filtered using Whatman filter paper to exclude bigger particles, diluted tenfold with deionized water, and then contaminated with CP at 5, 10, and 15 ppm concentrations. A 30-min incubation period was followed by analysis of the samples utilizing the N-CQDs fluorescence technique. The data presented in Table 1 shows that the recovery rates of CP varied between 102.4 % and 103.3 % in wastewater and between 103.6 % and 105.0 % in agricultural runoff. The results indicate that the fluorescence approach based on N-CQDs is very sensitive and efficient in detecting CP in complex environmental matrices, even after intrinsic CP content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization factor for photodegradation of CP
Influence of pollutant concentration, catalyst dose, and Ph on degradation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: An investigation was carried out to assess the degradation of CP using ZnHCF nanoparticles and N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposites. The study investigated different pollutant concentrations (ranging from 20 to 60 mgL−1), photocatalyst doses (ranging from 5 to 25 mg), pH values (ranging from 3 to 11), and illumination sources (sunlight, dark, and artificial white LED bulbs in a photoreactor chamber). Under sunlight exposure, the N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite demonstrated the most significant CP breakdown at a concentration of 20 mg L−1, reaching 91 % ( Fig. 5a-c). However, a decrease in the degradation capability of the N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite was observed with increasing pollutant content. The rise in pollutant concentration might reduce degradation efficiency due to the increased need for active species within the limited volume. The quantity of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) or free radicals remained consistent under specified radiation dosage and duration. The catalyst amount used significantly affects the removal efficiency [93].

Optimization showed that the N-CQDs@ZnHCF showed the highest degradation ability with a catalyst quantity of 15 mg, achieving 91 % CP degradation. However, an additional catalyst led to a weakening in the degradation curve. This decline in elimination efficacy beyond the 15 mg catalyst dose could be attributed to catalyst activity loss or molecule accumulation, reducing the overall sunlight availability and active surface area [94]. The photocatalyst molecules colliding with one another reduced the efficiency of the active sites. The N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite demonstrated the highest CP degradation efficiency of all the parent catalysts examined, demonstrating its superior photocatalytic efficacy (Fig. S6 a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of illumination source and N-CQDs concentration on photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite was systematically evaluated under different illumination conditions, including solar light, artificial white LED light, and dark conditions (Fig. S6 b). Under solar light, the nanocomposite achieved the highest CP removal efficiency (56–91 %) compared to artificial white LED light (34–37 %) and dark conditions (17–32 %). The superior photocatalytic performance under sunlight is attributed to the abundant energy available during daylight, which drives bandgap excitation on the nanoparticle surface, facilitating charge carrier excitation and subsequent pollutant degradation. Specifically, natural illumination effectively matches the low bandgap properties of the visible spectrum, enabling efficient energy transfer mechanisms for pollutant degradation. Additionally, sunlight is a cost-effective, environmentally safe, and readily available energy source compared to UV light, which may induce catalyst degradation or accelerate the breakdown of secondary contaminants [95]. These factors highlight the practical and sustainable advantages of sunlight for photocatalytic applications.

Further investigations focused on the effect of N-CQDs concentration in the N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite on CP degradation (Fig. S6 c). Experiments were conducted using a CP concentration of 20 mg L⁻¹ , an optimized catalyst dosage, neutral pH (7.0), and sunlight irradiation (452 ± 184 lux, 30 ± 1.7 °C) over 120 min. A control experiment with ZnHCF alone (0 mL N-CQDs) achieved a baseline degradation efficiency of 52 %, attributed to the inherent photocatalytic properties of ZnHCF. Incorporating 1 mL of N-CQDs significantly improved the degradation efficiency to 75 %, driven by enhanced visible light absorption and partial charge carrier separation. The maximum degradation efficiency of 91 % was achieved at 2 mL of N-CQDs, where an optimal balance of active sites, efficient charge separation, and elevated reactive oxygen species (ROS) production (e.g., •OH, •O₂⁻) was observed. However, increasing the concentration to 3 mL resulted in a decline in efficiency to 65 %, attributed to N-CQDs aggregation, which reduced the available active surface area and impeded charge transfer.

Mechanistic analysis reveals that incorporating N-CQDs into the ZnHCF matrix significantly enhances photocatalytic activity by reducing the bandgap, improving visible light absorption, and promoting pollutant adsorption via functional groups (-OH, -COOH). The observed concentration-dependent trends emphasize the necessity of optimizing N-CQDs content to achieve maximum photocatalytic efficiency. These findings demonstrate that both the illumination source and the concentration of N-CQDs are critical factors in modulating the photocatalytic degradation of CP, providing valuable insights into the development of highly efficient and sustainable nanocomposites for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs@ZnHCF in different wastewater samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical effectiveness of N-CQDs@ZnHCF as a photocatalyst for the degradation of CP from treated wastewater and agricultural runoff, specific volumes of treated wastewater (60 mL designated WWS and AWS, respectively) were collected from a local wastewater treatment facility after the completion of treatment processes, and agricultural runoff samples were obtained from nearby agricultural fields. Concentrations of CP were intentionally added to samples of treated WWS and AWS to replicate real-world ecological conditions. Before UV–vis examination, the samples underwent centrifugation to eliminate particulates. Multiple studies have shown that the absorbance of CP in water predominantly occurs at wavelengths of approximately 229 and 290 nm. The introduction of CP into samples was accomplished satisfactorily. After incorporating the ideal quantity of N-CQDs@ZnHCF (30 mg) into each sample, the mixtures were subjected to photocatalysis under sunlight. The temporal profile of absorbance in Fig. 5(e and f) UV-Vis spectra indicates the efficacy of CP degradation in the two water matrices. Following 120 min of exposure to sunlight, the absorbance peak at 229 and 290 nm in the AWS sample (Fig. 5e) diminishes, suggesting the breakage of CP bonds. Organic particles or other environmental factors may hinder the photocatalytic reduction of agricultural runoff. The WWS sample (Fig. 5 f) undergoes accelerated degradation at 229 and 290 nm wavelengths, resulting in substantial degradation within 40 min. Reducing the number of interfering components in the wastewater sample could enhance the efficiency of the N-CQDs@ZnHCF function. WWS degrades CP more rapidly than AWS, perhaps because of its less complex makeup. Analysis of the two samples' degradation rates reveals that the samples' composition is essential for evaluating photocatalytic treatments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics, adsorption isotherms, and photodegradation mechanism

ce:section.ce:para: Pollutants are initially adsorbed onto the surfaces of nanocatalysts, and then they are photodegraded by oxidative species produced by the nanocatalysts' semiconducting characteristics. These are the two basic steps involved in the removal of CP. Kinetic studies were carried out to comprehend the complexity and pace of adsorption. Pesticide molecules move in three stages: (i) migration around the adsorbent area, (ii) adhering to the surface, and (iii) infiltrating into the adsorbent's pores. The kinetic study of CP removal used optimized parameters for the nanocomposites N-CQDs@ZnCHF parent material ZnHCF, with a pollutant concentration of 20 mg/L, a catalyst amount of 15 mg, neutral pH, and exposure to sunlight. The rapid initial decline in the removal rate indicates that the early stage of the reaction was dominated by fast kinetics, likely due to the high availability of active sites and reactants. As the process progressed, the rate slowed, consistent with CP depletion and oxidative intermediates' formation, which aligns with first-order chemical kinetics (Fig. 5d). Ultimately, this led to the reduction of CP, driven by significant electron production.

Langmuir and Freundlich models were used to study the adsorption isotherm for the degradation of CP (Fig. S7 a and b). The Langmuir isotherm showed the most significant and favorable findings among the tested models. It had a high correlation coefficient (R2, 0.991) and a very low p-value (1.97 ×10−4) for the adsorption of CP, as illustrated in Table S3. The Langmuir-adsorption isotherm formulation was used to calculate the maximum adsorption capacity (Xm) for ZnHCF (0.377 mg−1) and N-CQDs@ZnHCF (0.73546 mg−1). Therefore, N-CQDs@ZnHCF is more effective in its maximum adsorption capacity, making it more suitable for the degradation and adsorption of CP.

Under optimized conditions, N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite efficiently degraded CP by 91 %, surpassing the parent nanomaterials. This enhanced efficiency is attributed to the stability of N-CQDs@ZnHCF, their ability to generate high levels of hydroxyl or free radicals, and reduced particle agglomeration. The process involves two steps: pollutant adsorption onto nanocatalyst surfaces and photocatalytic destruction simplified by the semiconductor characteristics of the nanocatalysts, producing oxidative species [96]. Adsorption includes medium resistance, mass transfer, catalyst surface adherence, and particle diffusion. Diffusion resistance affects mass transfer, influencing pollutant adsorption on nanocatalyst active sites. Photocatalytic removal generates active species like hydroxyl radicals and free radicals through initial adsorption, light absorption, and charge transfer [97]. N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposite accelerates adsorption, allowing CP molecules to reach active sites faster. Under sunlight exposure, the pollutant-coated photocatalyst triggers e- migration from VB to CB, generating "h+" in the VB [97]. Sunlight absorption leads to many photons, and the hybrid structure influences electron-hole lifetime, affecting charge separation dynamics in VB and CB (Fig. S8 a). Prolonging charge separation by adding N-CQDs to ZnHCF inhibits the recombination of holes (h+) and electrons (e-). Superoxide anions and hydroxyl radicals are produced when reactive electrons combine with oxygen, and hydroxyl radicals are produced when holes combine with water. Hydroperoxyl radicals are produced when O2 molecules and H⁺ interact [98]. The superoxide anion can also produce HOO and OH radicals [97]. The following equations can outline the overall mechanism for active radical generation and decay:

Our investigation aimed to identify the active, reactive species involved in the breakdown of CP (Fig. S6 d). Sunlight energizes electrons to higher levels, creating electron-hole pairs (e-/h+), which in turn generate reactive species such as •OH (hydroxyl radicals) by highly active positive holes reacting with H2O, and O2 •- (superoxide ions) formed when e- interacts with O2 molecules in water. Additionally, when O2 reacts with H+, hydroperoxyl radical (HOO•) is created and combined with •OH radicals, forming the superoxide anion. To control these electron-hole reactions, various quenchers like tert-butyl alcohol (t-BuOH), para-benzoquinone (pBZQ), and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (Na2EDTA) were used.

For the experiment, 10 mL solutions in vials with a pollutant concentration of 20 mg/L were prepared. 15 mg of photocatalyst and 2 mL of quenchers were added to each vial. The vials were then exposed to sunlight for 3 h. A control sample without a quencher was prepared for evaluation. Interestingly, superoxide ions (O2 •-) and holes (h+) play balancing roles in photocatalytic degradation, with hydroxyl radicals (•OH) being the primary active species responsible for CP degradation. Previous studies have highlighted the significant contribution of •OH in degrading organic pollutants using various nanocomposite materials such as CuO/ZnO nanorods, CuS/g-C₃N₄, BiVO₄/WO₃, and Fe₃O₄/h TiO₂/g-C₃N₄ [98–101].

GC-MS analysis was conducted to obtain mass spectra and total ion chromatograms (TIC) to suggest a viable pathway for eliminating CP. Fig. S9 presents the TIC and mass spectrum, respectively, illustrating the degraded metabolites of CP. As outlined in the pollution removal methodology, solar irradiation initiates the production of active species—these active species, particularly hydroxyl radicals generated after that, aid in removing CP. Drawing from the GC-MS findings, m/z values, and NIST spectra library, potential pathways for the degradation of CP are suggested [102].

The degradation pathway of CP pesticide involves a series of transformations resulting in specific metabolites (Fig. S8 b). Initially, CP (M.Wt 351) undergoes oxidation and dealkylation, leading to the formation of two primary products: O-3,5,6-trichloropyridin-2-yl O, O-dihydrogen phosphorothioate (m/z 294) and O-ethyl O-3,5,6-trihydroxypyridin-2-yl O-hydrogen phosphorothioate (m/z 267). The latter product (m/z 267) undergoes further oxidation, transforming the P = S group to a P = O group, resulting in 3,5,6-trihydroxypyridin-2-yl hydrogen phosphonate (m/z 207). This compound can hydrolyze into pyridine-2,3,5-triol (m/z 126) and H₂PO₃ and pyridine-3-ol (m/z 95). Further oxidation of O-3,5,6-trichloropyridin-2-yl O, O-dihydrogen phosphorothioate (m/z 294) produces (Z)-3-amino-2,3-dichloroacrylaldehyde (m/z 138). Eventually, these compounds are mineralized to H₂O and CO₂ [103–105].

The degradation process shows CP breaking down sequentially, producing intermediate compounds that mineralize. The recognized mechanism matches organophosphate pesticide breakdown studies that found similar oxidative and hydrolytic reactions [106]. Sun irradiation produces hydroxyl radicals that break down CP into harmless byproducts, suggesting that solar-powered advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) could reduce environmental deterioration [107]. Identifying intermediates like pyridine derivatives and acrylaldehydes helps explain the environmental and health effects of CP breakdown. In practical applications, these intermediates can indicate CP degradation effectiveness and thoroughness (Supporting Text 1.2 and Table S4)[108–112]. Further research should optimize degradation parameters to improve CP decomposition and reduce byproducts.

The performance of the N-CQDs@ZnHCF composite hinges on its efficiency, practicality, and recycling stability. Contaminated water was centrifuged to retrieve the nanocatalysts and filtered out. Acetone and deionized water were used for thorough cleaning, ensuring the removal of hazardous pollutants [113]. After multiple cleanings, the catalyst was dried at 50 °C for later use. Reusing the catalyst under optimized conditions reduced CP removal from 91 % to 72 %, likely due to CP accumulation without altering the catalyst's structure (Fig. S10 a). PXRD analysis after several cycles showed minimal changes, indicating high recyclability (Fig. S10 b). However, a slight decrease in efficiency after the 6th cycle suggests active site blockage by adsorbates or adsorbent inactivation during regeneration [114,115]. This underscores the long-lasting effectiveness of the nanocatalysts.

This study examined microwave-assisted carbonization of kinnow (Citrus reticulata) peels to produce N-CQDs. Nitrogen doping produced 4.45 nm-sized N-CQDs. HR-TEM, EDS, FE-SEM, and XRD validated their morphology, elemental content, and structural integrity. N-CQDs had a 44.48 % QY. Strong fluorescence emission made N-CQDs ideal for CP pesticide detection and showed a dynamic quenching mechanism upon CP binding. The LOD for CP is 0.084 ppm. Analysis of different wastewater samples detected pesticide residues with high sensitivity and recovery, with CP recovery rates ranging from 102.4 % to 103.3 % in wastewater and from 103.6 % to 105.0 % in agricultural runoff.

In the removal process, N-CQDs@ZnHCF nanocomposites successfully underwent a 91 % photocatalytic breakdown of CP when exposed to sunshine, emphasizing their promising prospects for practical environmental uses. The efficient generation of reactive species, such as hydroxyl radicals (OH) and superoxide anions (O₃⁺), was essential in maximizing the photocatalytic activity, which was particularly enhanced under natural sunlight circumstances. The improved degrading efficiency, particularly in wastewater, validates the practical usefulness of N-CQDs@ZnHCF for the purpose of environmental restoration. Their capacity to break down contaminants in both treated wastewater and agricultural runoff highlights the adaptability of these nanocomposites in different water matrices. Moreover, both N-CQDs and their ZnHCF composites exhibit considerable potential for identifying pesticides and wider applications in environmental sensing and remediation. These results establish the foundation for future nanomaterial-based initiatives to reduce agricultural pollution, providing a sustainable method for dealing with contamination in intricate water systems.
